content.full_text:
P,O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322-5662 
Administration 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
October 22, 1982 
Ms. Mary Ann Weems, Clerk 
City of Sand City 
1 Sylvan Park 
Sand City, CA 
93955 
Re: 
Request for Advice, Our No. A-82-l76 
Dear Ms. Weems: 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your recent communication requesting our 
advice. 
You have asked what steps a public official should 
take in order to properly accomplish a disqualification of 
himself or herself under the provisions of the Political 
Re form Act. * 
Section 87100 requires an official to disqualify 
himself or herself whenever he or she has reason to know 
that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. 
Section 87103 defines a financial interest as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans by a 
commercial lending institution in the regular course of 
buisness on terms available to the public without 
*Government Cod~ Sections 81000 -
91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. 


Ms. Mary Ann Weems, Clerk 
October 22, 1982 
Page Two 
regard to official status, aggregating two hundred 
fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, 
received by or promised to the public official within 
12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
For purposes of this section, indirect investment 
or interest means any investment or interest owned by 
the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by 
an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a 
business entity or trust in which the official, the 
official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own 
directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10% interest or 
greater. 
The Commission has adopted a regulation which spells 
out the procedure to be followed whenever an official has a 
financial interest in a decision which requires disquali-
fication. 
That regulation is 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700 (b) (5) 
(copy enclosed). 
That regulation requires that 
the official disclose the financial interest at the time of 
disqualification and that this disclosure be on the record 
in the case of a city councilmember or in writing to the 
supervisor in the case of a city employee. 
I hope that this answers your question, should you have 
additional questions, please feel free to contact this 
office at 916/322-5901. 
REL:km 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
j::tv-<-+-jct~· 
-, 'L 
Robert E. Leidigh 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
~

sections.question: What steps should a public official take in order to properly accomplish a disqualification of himself or herself under the provisions of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: The official must disclose the financial interest at the time of disqualification, and this disclosure must be on the record in the case of a city councilmember or in writing to the supervisor in the case of a city employee, pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(5).
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks what procedural steps a public official must follow to properly disqualify themselves from a decision under the Political Reform Act when they have a financial interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advises that officials must disqualify themselves when they have a financial interest as defined in Government Code Section 87103, and must disclose this interest either on the record (for councilmembers) or in writing to the supervisor (for employees), as required by the adopted regulation.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Document contains statutory definitions and regulatory citations. Minor typo noted in source document ('buisness' for 'business').

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What steps should a public official take in order to properly accomplish a disqualification of himself or herself under the provisions of the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: The official must disclose the financial interest at the time of disqualification, and this disclosure must be on the record in the case of a city councilmember or in writing to the supervisor in the case of a city employee, pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(5).

You have asked what steps a public official should take in order to properly accomplish a disqualification of himself or herself under the provisions of the Political Re form Act. * Section 87100 requires an official to disqualify himself or herself whenever he or she has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. Section 87103 defines a financial interest as follows: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). (d) Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public official, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a 10% interest or greater. embedding.summary: FPPC advises Ms. Weems, Clerk of Sand City, on the proper procedure for public officials to disqualify themselves from decisions involving financial interests under the Political Reform Act. The response explains the financial interest definitions, the disqualification requirement, and the required disclosure procedures.
